figures.caption: Figure 7. Neural correlates of Theory of Mind (ToM) ratings and associated temporal 565 response dynamics. (A) Cortical maps displaying significant positive zero-lag correlations 566 between canonical HRF-convolved ToM ratings and fMRI activity for The Present (TP), 567 Despicable Me (DM), and Partly Cloudy (PC). (B) Cortical maps displaying significant positive 568 zero-lag correlations between raw (unconvolved) ToM ratings and fMRI activity across the three 569 videos. (C) Cross-correlation matrix illustrating the relationship between the raw ToM rating and 570 fMRI time series across 114 regions of interest (ROIs) for movie TP across multiple temporal 571 lags. (D) Cortical maps illustrating significant regional responses to ToM ratings estimated via 572 Finite Impulse Response (FIR) deconvolution. (E) Estimated FIR response functions for 573 significant ROIs during movie TP, highlighting the rapid peak latencies. (F) Distributions of 574

Figure 5. Neural correlates of auditory pitch and temporal delay distributions. (A) Cortical 473 maps displaying positive zero-lag correlations between the canonical HRF-convolved auditory 474 pitch and fMRI activity during The Present (TP) and Despicable Me (DM). (B) Cortical maps 475 illustrating significant regional responses to auditory pitch estimated via Finite Impulse 476 Response (FIR) deconvolution for both videos. Statistical maps in panels A and B are 477 thresholded at a false-discovery rate (FDR) of p < 0.05. (C) Distributions of estimated temporal 478 delays (time-to-peak) for regions exhibiting positive FIR responses, categorized across Yeo’s 479 seven functional networks. Red markers denote two core auditory regions that are assigned to 480 the Default Mode Network in the Yeo parcellation. 481

(B) Cortical maps displaying significant positive418 zero-lag correlations between canonical HRF-convolved frame luminance (mean pixel intensity) 419 and fMRI activity for The Present (TP), DM, and Partly Cloudy (PC). Statistical maps in panel B 420 are thresholded at a false-discovery rate (FDR) of p < 0.05. (C) Estimated FIR response 421

Figure 2. Continuous time series of multimodal features associated with the movie The 253 Present. The waveforms illustrate the diverse macroscopic temporal properties across the five 254 feature classes. Low-level sensory features (frame luminance, frame contrast, and auditory 255 pitch) exhibit rapid, high-frequency fluctuations directly tied to instantaneous stimulus changes. 256 In contrast, the physiological (pupil size) and subjective cognitive (Theory of Mind [ToM] rating) 257 measures operate on noticeably slower, lower-frequency timescales, reflecting underlying 258 biological and cognitive integration delays. 259

Figure 4. Neural correlates of frame contrast and associated temporal response 442 dynamics. (A) Cross-correlation matrix illustrating the relationship between canonical HRF-443 convolved frame contrast and fMRI time series across 114 regions of interest (ROIs) for the 444 movie The Present (TP) across multiple temporal lags. (B) Cortical maps displaying significant 445 positive zero-lag correlations between canonical HRF-convolved frame contrast and fMRI 446 activity for TP, Despicable Me (DM), and Partly Cloudy (PC). (C) Cortical maps illustrating 447 significant regional responses to frame contrast estimated via Finite Impulse Response (FIR) 448 deconvolution. (D) Estimated FIR response functions across all 114 ROIs during movie TP, 449 reflecting the diversity of hemodynamic profiles. (E, F) Distributions of estimated temporal 450

Figure 1. Conceptual framework of nested temporal hierarchies and their mapping to 91 brain responses during naturalistic viewing. 92 Level 1: Intrinsic stimulus hierarchies. Naturalistic movies possess a deeply nested temporal 93 structure across modalities. Visual features (e.g., luminance, objects, actions, scenes) and 94 auditory/language features (e.g., pitch, phonemes, words, narratives) unfold over timescales 95 ranging from milliseconds to tens of seconds. The brain processes these continuous streams 96 through a topography of temporal receptive windows (TRWs), ranging from short integration 97 windows in primary sensory areas to extended timescales in higher-order networks. 98 Level 2: Observer-dependent delays. Extrinsic stimulus features elicit internally generated 99
